intro: AI is getting personal this week — Microsoft is reportedly coaching its sales team to trash-talk OpenAI and Anthropic, positioning its own models as the smarter, cheaper choice and signaling a dramatic shift in a partnership that once defined the AI race. Beyond the corporate drama, today's digest spans everything from a $20 million bet on AI for oil and gas plants to a psychologist's provocative argument that banning kids from social media is missing the point entirely. Grab your coffee and let's dig in.

stories.title: v2.1.211
stories.summary: Version 2.1.211 of Claude Code includes a new flag for forwarding subagent text in stream-json output, fixes for permission preview security issues involving bidirectional-override and special characters that could visually alter approval messages, fixes for auto mode incorrectly overriding PreToolUse hook 'ask' decisions for unsandboxed Bash commands, and fixes for parallel operations (text appears truncated).
stories.opinion: This is a meaningful patch release with important security fixes. The permission preview fix addressing bidirectional-override and zero-width characters is particularly notable as it prevents potential visual spoofing attacks in approval messages. The fix for auto mode respecting hook 'ask' decisions is also significant for users relying on hooks for safety controls. The new forward-subagent-text flag adds useful functionality for programmatic consumers. The changelog entry appears truncated, s…

stories.title: The social media ban sceptic: are we getting it wrong on kids, tech and mental health?
stories.summary: Psychologist Candice Odgers, who has studied adolescent mental health for 25 years, challenges the prevailing narrative around social media bans for children. She argues that the current debate around smartphones and tech obscures larger issues affecting teenagers, including the impact of Covid and the mental health of their adult caregivers. Odgers provocatively suggests that making the internet safer for children would be better achieved by removing adult men—the biggest perpetrators of sextortion and misinformation—rather than banning kids from platforms.
stories.opinion: This article raises an important and often overlooked counterpoint in the heated debate over youth social media use. Odgers' perspective is valuable because it redirects attention from simplistic bans toward the structural and systemic factors—such as pandemic fallout, caregiver mental health, and adult online behavior—that more significantly impact adolescent wellbeing. Her provocative suggestion about adult men highlights how the discourse tends to place responsibility on children rather than…

stories.title: Robots, AI and drones: how the Dutch navy is using tech to transform its sea defences
stories.summary: The article discusses how the Royal Netherlands Navy is integrating uncrewed systems, artificial intelligence, and drone technology into its sea defense operations. Captain Sjoerd Feenstra, head of the expertise centre for unmanned systems, is leading efforts to deploy autonomous vessels like Defender 1 and Defender 2, which serve as surveillance and monitoring platforms without crew onboard. The initiative aims to keep personnel out of danger zones while enhancing naval capabilities, positioning the Netherlands as a leader in military unmanned systems technology.
stories.opinion: This article highlights an important and inevitable trend in modern military operations: the shift toward autonomous and unmanned systems. The Dutch navy's proactive approach to integrating these technologies seems strategically sound, as it can reduce risk to human life while potentially expanding operational capabilities. However, the article raises broader questions about the ethics and accountability of autonomous military systems, the potential for escalation in arms races involving AI-dri…

stories.title: Applied Computing wants to give oil and gas operators an AI model for the entire plant
stories.summary: Applied Computing, a London-based startup founded in 2023, has raised a $20 million Series A led by engineering giant KBR with Databricks Ventures participating. The company is building a foundation AI model called Orbital for the oil, gas, and petrochemical industry. Orbital combines time series, physics-based, and language models to predict facility states by analyzing sensor readings, physics/chemistry constraints, and equipment parameters in real time. The startup claims facilities currently use less than 8% of available data for operating decisions, and Orbital can compress investigations from days or weeks into seconds. Applied Computing reports reaching double-digit millions in annual recurring revenue within 18 months of leaving stealth. Partners include Wipro and KBR, which has integrated Orbital into its INSITE 3.0 digital platform. The company competes with established player…
stories.opinion: This is a compelling startup tackling a genuine pain point in industrial operations. The combination of time series, physics-based, and language models into a unified foundation model is a smart architectural choice that differentiates it from both traditional simulation software and generic AI tools. The revenue traction — double-digit millions in ARR in under 18 months — is impressive and suggests real product-market fit. KBR leading the round as a strategic investor adds credibility and prov…

stories.title: Will the new AI roadmap keep the tech giants in line? | Fiona Katauskas
stories.summary: This Guardian article features a political cartoon by Fiona Katauskas commenting on a new AI roadmap and whether it will effectively regulate tech giants or if they will chart their own course. The cartoon is tagged under the topics of datacentres and AI (artificial intelligence). The article itself is primarily visual commentary with minimal text beyond the headline question.
stories.opinion: The cartoon's framing — asking whether the AI roadmap will 'keep the tech giants in line' or if they'll 'forge a path of their own' — strongly implies skepticism that regulatory efforts will successfully constrain major technology companies. The use of a political cartoon format suggests a critical, satirical stance toward both the adequacy of government AI regulation and the willingness of tech giants to comply with oversight measures.

stories.title: Anthony Albanese’s AI vision scores high on vibes but the devil will be in the detail. And there is one glaring omission … | David Pocock
stories.summary: David Pocock critiques Prime Minister Anthony Albanese's AI vision speech delivered at the University of Sydney, noting it was high on rhetoric but lacked policy detail. The article highlights concerns about ambiguity in proposed laws for 'next generation of large-scale datacentres' and notes calls for a datacentre moratorium until new regulations are established. Pocock identifies a glaring omission in the plan, though the full article content is truncated.
stories.opinion: The author, Senator David Pocock, is critical of Albanese's AI announcement, viewing it as heavy on aspirational language ('vibes') but insufficient on concrete policy specifics. Pocock appears skeptical about the government's approach, particularly regarding datacentre regulation, and seems to advocate for stronger, more detailed regulatory frameworks before proceeding with AI infrastructure expansion.

stories.title: Microsoft is reportedly training salespeople to talk down OpenAI and Anthropic
stories.summary: Microsoft is reportedly training its sales team to negatively compare AI products from OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic to its own in-house models, emphasizing efficiency and cost-effectiveness. At an internal strategy meeting, executives pitched Microsoft as offering a 'full end-to-end system' versus competitors selling 'parts.' Executive VP Jacob Andreou directly compared Copilot to Anthropic's Claude, calling it 'slower and less accurate' with lacking security integrations. This follows reports that Microsoft has been replacing OpenAI's and Anthropic's models in flagship apps like Word and Excel with its own models to cut costs. The shift is notable given Microsoft's long-standing partnership with OpenAI, which was amended in April to drop exclusivity clauses. The competitive positioning appears aimed at addressing investor concerns about Microsoft's massive AI spending and demonstratin…
stories.opinion: This is a significant but entirely predictable development in the AI industry's maturation. Microsoft investing billions in OpenAI while simultaneously building competing in-house models was always going to lead to this kind of tension. The fact that they're now actively training salespeople to trash-talk their own partner's products signals that the cozy Microsoft-OpenAI relationship is functionally over in all but name. From a competitive standpoint, Microsoft's pitch about being an 'end-to-e…

stories.title: Sheetz is quitting VMware, migrating 11,000 virtual machines
stories.summary: Sheetz, a US convenience store chain with 838 locations, is migrating approximately 11,000 virtual machines from VMware vSphere to StorMagic's SvHCI platform. The move was prompted by Broadcom's acquisition of VMware and subsequent changes including eliminating perpetual licenses in favor of mandatory subscription bundles with five-year commitments, creating budget uncertainty. Sheetz has completed migration at over 600 stores, averaging 200 per month, and expects to finish within four months. The company is leveraging its existing relationship with StorMagic, having used their SvSAN product since 2019, and is performing the migration remotely without hardware upgrades, saving significant costs. Challenges included operating in a 24/7 retail environment requiring heavy automation and meticulous planning to minimize disruption, as well as working with SvHCI's still-maturing APIs.
stories.opinion: This is a significant real-world example of the VMware exodus that Broadcom's aggressive licensing changes have triggered. Sheetz's case is particularly compelling because it demonstrates that even large-scale migrations with thousands of VMs across hundreds of distributed locations are feasible — not just for centralized data centers. The fact that they're averaging 200 stores per month and doing it remotely without hardware changes undermines the narrative that VMware lock-in is inescapable.…

tweet_headlines.author: Zephyr_hg
tweet_headlines.headline: Zephyr breaks down the agentic loop pattern: design it once, __let it self-check and produce__ without re-prompting.
tweet_headlines.source_text:
This is a loop: you design it once, and it keeps producing without you re-prompting it.

It runs, it checks its own work, and it hands you the finished thing.

You stay the judge. The loop does the grinding.

One person with a few of these produces what a payroll used to buy. https://t.co/6KFpek4SfA https://t.co/yePkoc2Mjq

tweet_headlines.author: thisiskp_
tweet_headlines.headline: Screenpipe flips the agent-building question: stop describing your workflow to AI and __let it watch you work__ instead.
tweet_headlines.source_text:
Here’s the thing: 

Everyone building agents has been starting with the same question: how do I describe my workflow to an AI?

Screenpipe flips it. You have to describe your work. Just let it watch.

Record how you work → searchable memory → SOPs → agents. Your workflow https://t.co/K4TMmuftU0

tweet_headlines.author: realcolegordon
tweet_headlines.headline: Daniel Fazio on the biggest AI agency mistake: saying "I can automate your business" __means a million things and nobody bites__.
tweet_headlines.source_text:
Daniel Fazio on the biggest mistake people make trying to start an AI automation agency:

"Everyone's trying to do outbound. Stop. The problem is when you say 'I can automate your business,' that means a million and a half things. Nobody bites. There's one thing you can do https://t.co/imab5yMRo2 https://t.co/S4mSo4zP2t
